name: Liberia
tagline: From the Kru, Grebo, and Vai of the Grain Coast to a settler republic of freed African Americans, the long rule of the Americo-Liberian elite, two devastating civil wars, and Africa's first elected woman president.
summary: The land that is Liberia today was long home to a mosaic of indigenous peoples — Mande speakers such as the Vai and Kpelle, Kwa speakers such as the Kru, Grebo, and Bassa, and the Mel-speaking Gola and Kisi — whose societies traded along the West African coast that Europeans called the Grain or Pepper Coast. From 1822 the American Colonization Society, an American body that sought to resettle free-born and freed African Americans in Africa, planted settlers at Cape Mesurado, founding the town that became Monrovia. On 26 July 1847 these settlers proclaimed the Republic of Liberia, the first modern republic in Africa, governed under a constitution modelled on that of the United States. For more than a century the Americo-Liberian settler minority, organised in the True Whig Party, dominated political and economic life and largely excluded the indigenous majority. In 1980 a coup by Master Sergeant Samuel Doe ended Americo-Liberian rule, and from 1989 the country was torn apart by two civil wars, associated above all with the warlord and later president Charles Taylor. After peace in 2003, Liberians elected Ellen Johnson Sirleaf in 2005 — Africa's first elected woman head of state — and the nation later weathered the catastrophic 2014 Ebola epidemic.

founding.label: Declaration of the Republic of Liberia
founding.detail: On 26 July 1847 delegates representing the settler communities along the coast issued the Liberian Declaration of Independence and proclaimed the Republic of Liberia, making it the first modern sovereign republic on the African continent. The settlers — free-born and formerly enslaved African Americans brought out from the United States from 1822 under the auspices of the American Colonization Society, together with 'recaptives' freed from intercepted slave ships — had outgrown rule by the Society and faced challenges to their authority from European traders. Their constitution, drafted with the help of the Harvard jurist Simon Greenleaf and modelled closely on that of the United States, established a republican government, and Joseph Jenkins Roberts, a free man of colour born in Norfolk, Virginia, became the new republic's first president in 1848. Britain recognised Liberia in 1848 and France in 1852, but the United States, distracted by the slavery question, withheld formal recognition until 1862. The republic so proclaimed rested on a stark imbalance: a small Americo-Liberian settler class held citizenship and power, while the indigenous African majority on whose land the state was built were long treated as subjects rather than equals.

quickFacts.label: Independence
quickFacts.value: Republic of Liberia declared on 26 July 1847; Africa's first modern republic

quickFacts.label: Founded by
quickFacts.value: The American Colonization Society, which settled freed and free-born African Americans from 1822 at Cape Mesurado

quickFacts.label: Capital
quickFacts.value: Monrovia, named after U.S. President James Monroe; founded 1822 as Christopolis

quickFacts.label: Indigenous peoples
quickFacts.value: Mande (Vai, Kpelle), Kwa (Kru, Grebo, Bassa, Krahn), and Mel (Gola, Kisi) groups; the Vai devised their own syllabic script

quickFacts.label: Settler rule
quickFacts.value: The Americo-Liberian elite and the True Whig Party dominated politics from independence until the 1980 coup

quickFacts.label: Civil wars
quickFacts.value: Two civil wars, 1989–1997 and 1999–2003, killed an estimated 200,000–250,000 people

eras.title: Indigenous peoples of the Grain Coast
eras.standfirst: Long before the settlers came, the forests and coast were home to Mande, Kwa, and Mel-speaking peoples — among them the Kru, Grebo, and the Vai, who created one of Africa's few indigenous scripts.
body: The territory of modern Liberia was settled over many centuries by a diversity of African peoples, whose languages all belong to the Niger-Congo family and are conventionally grouped into three clusters. The Mande speakers of the north and west include the Vai and the Kpelle, the country's largest single group; the Kwa speakers of the south and coast include the Bassa, the Kru, the Grebo, and the Krahn; and the Mel or southern Atlantic speakers include the Gola and the Kisi, often described as among the oldest inhabitants of the region. These societies were organised in towns, chiefdoms, and confederations, farming rice and cassava, working iron, and trading along the rivers and the sea.

European mariners reached this coast in the fifteenth and sixteenth centuries and named its stretches for the goods they sought: the Grain Coast or Pepper Coast, after the melegueta pepper, or 'grains of paradise', gathered there. Coastal peoples such as the Kru and Grebo became renowned seafarers and traders, working aboard European ships and as labourers up and down the West African coast, while the interior peoples supplied forest products, ivory, and — as elsewhere in the region — captives to the Atlantic trade.

Among the Vai of the northwest arose one of the very few writing systems invented in sub-Saharan Africa. The Vai syllabary is traditionally credited to Momolu Duwalu Bukele and a circle of elders, who are thought to have devised it around 1832–1833 (some accounts give earlier dates), reportedly after a dream. Schools were set up to teach it, and the script — still in use today — became a celebrated symbol of indigenous literacy and a subject of fascination for later scholars of how writing systems are created.

events.title: Settlement by Mande, Kwa, and Mel peoples
events.summary: Over centuries the region was settled by Niger-Congo-speaking peoples — Mande groups such as the Vai and Kpelle, Kwa groups such as the Kru, Grebo, and Bassa, and Mel groups such as the Gola and Kisi, the last regarded as among the oldest inhabitants.

events.title: Europeans name the Grain Coast
events.summary: Portuguese and other European mariners charted the coast and called it the Grain or Pepper Coast after the melegueta pepper traded there; Kru and Grebo seafarers became known along the West African coast.

events.title: Invention of the Vai script
events.summary: Momolu Duwalu Bukele and fellow Vai elders are credited with devising the Vai syllabary, one of the few indigenous writing systems created in sub-Saharan Africa, which remains in use; some accounts place its origin earlier.

pullquote.text: The Vai syllabary is one of the very few writing systems ever invented in sub-Saharan Africa — and it is still in use today.

eras.title: The American Colonization Society settlement
eras.standfirst: From 1822 the American Colonization Society planted settlers of free-born and freed African Americans at Cape Mesurado, founding the town that became Monrovia.
body: The American Colonization Society was founded in the United States in 1816 by a coalition with very mixed motives: some members were genuine opponents of slavery who believed free black Americans would never find equality in the United States, while others were slaveholders who wished to remove free people of colour from the country. The Society proposed to resettle free-born and emancipated African Americans on the West African coast. After a failed first attempt at Sherbro Island, Society agents — backed by the threat of force from a U.S. naval officer — secured Cape Mesurado from local Dey and Bassa rulers in 1821.

In 1822 the first settlers went ashore and established a foothold at Cape Mesurado, on land at the mouth of the Mesurado River. The settlement, at first called Christopolis, was renamed Monrovia in 1824 in honour of the U.S. president James Monroe, a supporter of the Society, and the colony itself was named Liberia, from the Latin for 'free'. The white agent Jehudi Ashmun did much to organise its early government, defence, and commerce. Over the following decades several thousand settlers arrived, joined by 'recaptives' or 'Congoes' — Africans freed by American patrols from intercepted slave ships and landed in Liberia.

The settlers built a society that consciously mirrored the American South they had left, with churches, plantations, and an English-speaking Christian culture, while their relations with the surrounding indigenous peoples were often tense and at times violent. Sustaining the colony strained the Society's finances, and the settlers increasingly resented its control and the refusal of European traders to recognise their authority to govern and levy customs. By the 1840s the demand for full self-government had become irresistible.

events.title: Founding of the American Colonization Society
events.summary: The American Colonization Society was established in the United States to resettle free-born and freed African Americans in West Africa, drawing together abolitionists and slaveholders with sharply differing motives.

events.title: Acquisition of Cape Mesurado
events.summary: Society agents, backed by a U.S. naval officer, secured Cape Mesurado from local rulers as the site for the colony, after an earlier attempt to settle at Sherbro Island had failed.

events.title: First settlers land and found the colony
events.summary: Free-born and freed African American settlers went ashore at Cape Mesurado and established the settlement of Christopolis, the nucleus of the colony of Liberia, with the agent Jehudi Ashmun organising its early administration.

events.title: Christopolis renamed Monrovia
events.summary: The settlement was renamed Monrovia after U.S. President James Monroe, a patron of the Colonization Society, and the colony was named Liberia, 'land of the free'.

figures.name: Jehudi Ashmun
figures.blurb: White American agent who organised the early government, defence, and commerce of the Liberia colony in the 1820s and is often described as a founder of the settlement.

pullquote.text: The settlement was named Monrovia after a sitting American president, and the colony Liberia — 'land of the free'.

eras.title: The republic and the True Whig order
eras.standfirst: On 26 July 1847 the settlers proclaimed Africa's first modern republic — but power rested with the Americo-Liberian minority and the True Whig Party, while a 99-year concession in 1926 handed Firestone vast plantations.
body: On 26 July 1847 the settlers proclaimed the Republic of Liberia, adopting a constitution modelled on that of the United States and drafted with the help of the Harvard jurist Simon Greenleaf. Joseph Jenkins Roberts, born free in Virginia, was inaugurated as the first president in 1848. Liberia thus became the first modern republic in Africa and a symbol, for many in the African diaspora, of black self-government — even as Europe recognised it more readily than the slaveholding United States, which withheld recognition until 1862.

Political life was dominated by the Americo-Liberian settlers and their descendants, a minority of perhaps a few percent of the population. From 1869 the True Whig Party came to monopolise office, and from 1878 it governed virtually unchallenged for more than a century — one of the longest single-party dominances anywhere in the world. The indigenous African majority, who lived in the interior brought only gradually under Monrovia's control, were largely excluded from citizenship and power and at times subjected to forced labour; an international scandal over labour practices forced President Charles D. B. King from office in 1930.

Chronically short of revenue, the republic leaned heavily on foreign capital. In 1926 the True Whig government granted the American Firestone Tire and Rubber Company a 99-year concession to lease up to a million acres — establishing what became one of the world's largest rubber plantations and binding Liberia's economy tightly to a single foreign corporation and a single commodity. The arrangement brought investment and a measure of stability but also deep dependence and controversy over its labour conditions.

events.title: The Republic of Liberia is proclaimed
events.summary: Delegates issued the Liberian Declaration of Independence and proclaimed the Republic of Liberia under a U.S.-modelled constitution, making it the first modern republic in Africa.

events.title: Joseph Jenkins Roberts inaugurated
events.summary: Joseph Jenkins Roberts, a free man of colour born in Virginia, was inaugurated as the first president of the Republic of Liberia.

events.title: United States recognises Liberia
events.summary: Having delayed during the era of slavery, the United States formally recognised the Republic of Liberia in 1862 and concluded a treaty of commerce and navigation with it.

events.title: True Whig Party dominance begins
events.summary: From 1878 the True Whig Party, the party of the Americo-Liberian elite, held power virtually without interruption for more than a century, marginalising the indigenous majority.

events.title: The Firestone rubber concession
events.summary: The government granted the Firestone Tire and Rubber Company a 99-year concession to lease up to a million acres, creating one of the world's largest rubber plantations and binding Liberia's economy to a single foreign company.

figures.name: Joseph Jenkins Roberts
figures.blurb: Virginia-born free man of colour who became the first president of the Republic of Liberia in 1848 and a leading figure of the early Americo-Liberian state.

pullquote.text: Liberia became the first modern republic in Africa — yet for over a century its power rested with a settler minority and a single party.

eras.title: The Tubman era and the 1980 coup
eras.standfirst: William Tubman opened Liberia to foreign investment and began to extend the franchise — but the old settler order endured until Master Sergeant Samuel Doe seized power in 1980, ending 133 years of Americo-Liberian rule.
body: William V. S. Tubman, a True Whig politician from the Maryland County settler community, served as president from 1944 until his death in 1971 — more than a quarter of a century. He is often called the father of modern Liberia for two signature policies. His 'Open Door' policy courted foreign investment and channelled hundreds of millions of dollars, chiefly American, into rubber, iron ore, and infrastructure, fuelling rapid economic growth. His 'National Unification' policy aimed, at least rhetorically, to integrate the indigenous interior with the settler-dominated coast, and saw the franchise extended to more indigenous Liberians and some brought into government, even as the True Whig system and Tubman's own dominance remained intact.

Tubman was succeeded in 1971 by his vice-president, William R. Tolbert, who promised reform but presided over rising discontent as economic inequality, corruption, and the entrenched privileges of the Americo-Liberian elite fuelled opposition. Protests over a proposed rise in the price of rice in 1979 turned deadly and shook the regime.

On 12 April 1980 a group of soldiers led by Master Sergeant Samuel K. Doe, of the indigenous Krahn people, stormed the Executive Mansion and killed President Tolbert. The coup ended 133 years of Americo-Liberian and True Whig rule and brought to power, for the first time, a government led from the indigenous majority. Days later thirteen senior officials of the ousted government were publicly executed on a Monrovia beach. Doe's military People's Redemption Council ruled by decree; a deeply flawed election in 1985 confirmed him as president, but his rule grew increasingly violent and tilted toward his own Krahn community, sowing the resentments that would soon erupt in civil war.

events.title: William Tubman becomes president
events.summary: William V. S. Tubman took office as president and would govern until 1971, launching the 'Open Door' policy of foreign investment and the 'National Unification' policy toward the indigenous interior.

events.title: Tolbert succeeds Tubman
events.summary: On Tubman's death his vice-president William R. Tolbert became president, promising reform but facing mounting discontent over inequality and Americo-Liberian privilege.

events.title: The rice riots
events.summary: Protests in Monrovia against a proposed increase in the price of rice turned deadly, exposing the fragility of the Tolbert government and the depth of popular grievance.

events.title: Samuel Doe's coup ends settler rule
events.summary: Master Sergeant Samuel K. Doe led soldiers who killed President Tolbert and seized power, ending 133 years of Americo-Liberian and True Whig dominance; thirteen officials were soon publicly executed.

events.title: Doe confirmed in a flawed election
events.summary: Samuel Doe was declared the winner of a widely criticised election, formalising his presidency; his increasingly repressive and ethnically partisan rule deepened tensions across Liberia.

figures.name: William V. S. Tubman
figures.blurb: Long-serving True Whig president, often called the father of modern Liberia, whose 'Open Door' and 'National Unification' policies courted foreign investment and began, cautiously, to extend rights to the indigenous majority.

figures.name: Samuel K. Doe
figures.role: Military ruler and President of Liberia
figures.blurb: Krahn master sergeant who seized power in the 1980 coup, ending Americo-Liberian rule, then governed with growing violence until he was captured and killed in 1990 at the start of the civil war.

pullquote.text: The 1980 coup ended 133 years of Americo-Liberian rule — and, for the first time, brought the indigenous majority to power.

eras.title: The civil wars and Charles Taylor
eras.standfirst: Two civil wars between 1989 and 2003 killed an estimated 200,000 or more Liberians; their central figure, Charles Taylor, ruled as president and was later convicted of war crimes for backing atrocities in Sierra Leone.
body: On 24 December 1989 the National Patriotic Front of Liberia (NPFL), led by Charles Taylor, launched an invasion from Côte d'Ivoire, igniting the First Liberian Civil War. The conflict fractured along factional and ethnic lines and was marked by massacres, the widespread use of child soldiers, and the collapse of the state. President Samuel Doe was captured and killed by a rival faction in 1990. A West African intervention force, ECOMOG, deployed to contain the fighting, but the war dragged on through years of failed ceasefires until a 1996 peace agreement led to elections in 1997, which Taylor won amid an atmosphere of intimidation and war-weariness.

Peace did not hold. From 1999 a new rebellion by Liberians United for Reconciliation and Democracy (LURD), and later a second group, MODEL, opened the Second Liberian Civil War against Taylor's government. By 2003 the rebels had besieged Monrovia itself. Under intense international and regional pressure, and indicted by a UN-backed court for his role in neighbouring Sierra Leone, Taylor resigned and went into exile in Nigeria in August 2003. A comprehensive peace agreement signed in Accra ended the war, and a large United Nations peacekeeping mission, UNMIL, deployed to secure the fragile peace.

Across the fourteen years of conflict, estimates commonly place the death toll at around 200,000 to 250,000 people, with much of the population displaced. The wars were notorious for atrocities against civilians. Taylor was later surrendered to the Special Court for Sierra Leone; in 2012 it convicted him of aiding and abetting war crimes and crimes against humanity — including murder, rape, and the use of child soldiers — committed by rebels he supported in Sierra Leone, and sentenced him to 50 years in prison, making him the first former head of state convicted by an international tribunal since Nuremberg.

events.title: First Liberian Civil War begins
events.summary: Charles Taylor's National Patriotic Front of Liberia invaded from Côte d'Ivoire, beginning a brutal civil war marked by ethnic massacres and the widespread use of child soldiers.

events.title: Samuel Doe captured and killed
events.summary: President Samuel Doe was captured, tortured, and killed by a rival rebel faction, and a West African ECOMOG force deployed in an attempt to contain the spreading conflict.

events.title: Charles Taylor elected president
events.summary: After a 1996 peace accord, Charles Taylor won the 1997 presidential election amid intimidation and war-weariness, ending the first civil war but not the underlying conflict.

events.title: Second Liberian Civil War begins
events.summary: The rebel group LURD launched a new insurgency against Taylor's government from the north, opening a second civil war that would eventually besiege Monrovia.

events.title: Taylor resigns; Accra peace agreement
events.summary: With rebels besieging Monrovia and an international indictment over Sierra Leone hanging over him, Charles Taylor resigned and went into exile; a comprehensive peace agreement in Accra ended the war and a UN mission, UNMIL, deployed.

events.title: Taylor convicted of war crimes
events.summary: The Special Court for Sierra Leone convicted Charles Taylor of aiding and abetting war crimes and crimes against humanity in Sierra Leone and sentenced him to 50 years — the first former head of state convicted by an international tribunal since Nuremberg.

figures.name: Charles Taylor
figures.blurb: Founder of the NPFL who launched the 1989 invasion, served as president from 1997, resigned into exile in 2003, and was convicted in 2012 by the Special Court for Sierra Leone for backing atrocities in that country's war.

pullquote.text: Convicted of aiding war crimes in Sierra Leone, Charles Taylor became the first former head of state sentenced by an international tribunal since Nuremberg.
pullquote.attribution: The Special Court for Sierra Leone, 2012

eras.title: Peace, Sirleaf, and the Ebola epidemic
eras.standfirst: After the 2003 peace, Liberians elected Ellen Johnson Sirleaf in 2005 — Africa's first elected woman head of state — and the nation later endured the devastating 2014 Ebola epidemic.
body: The 2003 Accra peace agreement and the deployment of the United Nations Mission in Liberia (UNMIL) opened a long, difficult recovery. A transitional government gave way to landmark elections in 2005. In the run-off, Ellen Johnson Sirleaf, a Harvard-trained economist and former finance official, defeated the popular footballer George Weah, and in January 2006 she was inaugurated as Liberia's president — the first woman elected to lead an African nation. In 2011 she shared the Nobel Peace Prize for her work on women's rights and peacebuilding.

In 2014 Liberia was struck by the West African Ebola epidemic, the largest outbreak of the disease in history. The virus overwhelmed a health system still shattered by war; by the time Liberia was declared free of active transmission, the country had recorded thousands of deaths — the heaviest toll of the three worst-affected nations — and the epidemic left lasting scars on its economy and society. The international and community response, mobilised under Sirleaf's government, was eventually credited with halting the outbreak.

Sirleaf served two terms and in 2018 handed power to George Weah, who had won the 2017 election — the country's first peaceful, democratic transfer of power between elected presidents in decades. Weah was in turn defeated by Joseph Boakai in the 2023 election, another orderly handover. More than two decades after the guns fell silent, Liberia remains a poor country still reckoning with the legacy of its wars, but its peace has endured and its democratic institutions have repeatedly proven able to transfer power at the ballot box.

events.title: Ellen Johnson Sirleaf elected
events.summary: Ellen Johnson Sirleaf won the presidential run-off against George Weah and was inaugurated in January 2006 as the first woman elected to lead an African nation.

events.title: Sirleaf shares the Nobel Peace Prize
events.summary: Ellen Johnson Sirleaf was awarded the Nobel Peace Prize, jointly with two other women, for non-violent work on behalf of women's safety and participation in peacebuilding.

events.title: The Ebola epidemic
events.summary: Liberia was among the countries worst hit by the West African Ebola epidemic, the largest in history, which killed thousands and overwhelmed a health system already weakened by war before transmission was halted.

events.title: Peaceful transfer to George Weah
events.summary: Sirleaf handed power to George Weah, who won the 2017 election — Liberia's first peaceful democratic transfer of power between elected presidents in over seven decades.

events.title: Boakai defeats Weah
events.summary: Joseph Boakai defeated the incumbent George Weah in the 2023 election and took office in 2024, marking another orderly transfer of power in post-war Liberia.

figures.name: Ellen Johnson Sirleaf
figures.blurb: Harvard-trained economist who in 2005 became the first woman elected to lead an African nation, led Liberia's post-war recovery and the response to the 2014 Ebola epidemic, and shared the 2011 Nobel Peace Prize.

pullquote.text: In 2005 Liberians elected Ellen Johnson Sirleaf — the first woman ever elected to lead an African nation.

figures.name: Joseph Jenkins Roberts
figures.blurb: Virginia-born free man of colour who became the first president of the Republic of Liberia in 1848, after its declaration of independence in 1847.

figures.name: William V. S. Tubman
figures.blurb: Long-serving president, often called the father of modern Liberia, whose 'Open Door' and 'National Unification' policies courted investment and began to extend rights to the indigenous majority.

figures.name: Ellen Johnson Sirleaf
figures.blurb: Economist who in 2005 became Africa's first elected woman head of state, led post-war recovery and the Ebola response, and shared the 2011 Nobel Peace Prize.
